steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: What does a voltmeter measure?
options: The current flowing through a component
The energy transferred per unit charge across a component
The total energy used by the circuit
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! A voltmeter measures potential difference (voltage) — the energy transferred per unit of charge between two points. It is connected in parallel, not in series. It does not measure current or resistance directly.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Potential difference (voltage) is the energy transferred per coulomb of charge that passes between two points. A voltmeter is always connected in parallel across a component.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.2.1 — Potential difference

steps.title: Potential Difference
steps.bodyHTML: Potential difference (p.d. or voltage) is the energy transferred per unit charge that passes between two points. It tells you how much energy each coulomb of charge transfers as it moves through a component. A voltmeter is connected in parallel across a component to measure p.d.

relationships: Bigger E → bigger V (more energy per charge)
Bigger Q → smaller V (energy shared between more charge)

commonErrors: Connecting a voltmeter in series instead of in parallel
Forgetting that p.d. is energy per UNIT charge, not total energy

steps.title: See it in action
steps.instruction: Drag the sliders to change energy and charge. Watch how potential difference responds.

aqa.questionText: A battery transfers 36 J of energy when 3.0 C of charge flows. Calculate the potential difference across the battery.

steps.label: Identify the knowns
steps.content:
energy E = 36 J
charge Q = 3.0 C

steps.label: Apply V = E / Q
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — correct equation; 1 mark — correct substitution; 1 mark — correct answer + unit

edexcel.questionText: A component transfers 240 J of energy when 20 C of charge passes through it. Calculate the potential difference.

steps.label: Apply V = E / Q
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark equation; 1 mark substitution; 1 mark answer + unit

ocr-gateway.questionText: A bulb has a potential difference of 6.0 V across it. 5.0 C of charge flows through the bulb. How much energy is transferred?

steps.label: Rearrange for E
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — rearrangement; 1 mark — correct answer + unit

ocr-21c.questionText: A 9.0 V battery delivers 4.5 C of charge. Calculate the energy transferred.

steps.label: Apply E = QV
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — substitution; 1 mark — correct answer + unit

wjec.questionText: A lamp has a p.d. of 12 V. 8.0 C of charge flows. Calculate the energy transferred.

steps.label: Apply E = QV
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — substitution; 1 mark — correct answer + unit

aqa.questionText: A heater transfers 600 J of energy when 50 C of charge flows through it. What is the potential difference across the heater?
aqa.markSchemeAward: 1 mark equation · 1 mark substitution · 1 mark correct answer + unit
aqa.commonMistake: Option A is a trap — multiplying instead of dividing gives 30 000.

edexcel.questionText: A motor transfers 180 J when 15 C of charge flows. Calculate the p.d.
edexcel.markSchemeAward: 1 mark rearrangement · 1 mark correct answer + unit

ocr-gateway.questionText: A 230 V mains supply delivers 10 C of charge. How much energy is transferred?
ocr-gateway.markSchemeAward: 1 mark substitution · 1 mark correct answer + unit

ocr-21c.questionText: A 6.0 V battery transfers 48 J of energy. How much charge flowed?
ocr-21c.markSchemeAward: 1 mark rearrangement · 1 mark correct answer + unit

wjec.questionText: A 3.0 V cell delivers 15 C. Calculate the energy transferred.
wjec.markSchemeAward: 1 mark equation · 1 mark correct answer + unit

equation.examTip: Remember: potential difference is energy per unit charge. 1 volt = 1 joule per coulomb.

boardNotes.aqa: On AQA formula sheet. Often rearranged — practise all three forms.
